title: Program: Build the learning-english MVP from the docs-first architecture
description:
This epic is the program-level container for turning the current docs-first repository into a production-minded English-learning platform.

The repository already contains a detailed architecture in PLAN.md, but no implementation scaffold yet. This issue exists so future work has a single top-level anchor that explains the purpose of the backlog and how the dependency graph is intended to be read.

This epic should remain open until the project has a functioning end-to-end MVP that covers the core loop:
import -> validate -> commit -> publish -> learner consume -> progress/retry-safe writes.
design:
- Mirror the roadmap in PLAN.md so the backlog and architecture tell the same story.
- Use epics for phases, parent-child links for scope ownership, and explicit blockers for technical sequencing.
- Store intent in issue fields so future contributors can onboard from Beads without reconstructing context manually.
- Keep the MVP loop central: import -> validate -> commit -> publish -> learner consume -> retry-safe progress.
acceptance_criteria:
- Every major roadmap phase from PLAN.md is represented as an epic.
- Critical invariants are captured in the backlog, not only in prose docs.
- Dependencies make build order and intended parallelism visible.
- The backlog is detailed enough to serve as a self-documenting execution plan.
notes:
- Current repo reality is docs-first with no implementation scaffold yet.
- Scope discipline matters: post-MVP ideas stay visible without diluting the MVP path.
- Publication safety, idempotency, deterministic sessions, and secure session handling are foundational constraints.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Backlog map:
- learning-english-2a6.1: delivery foundation and scaffold
- learning-english-2a6.2: auth and session foundation
- learning-english-2a6.3: content operations and publication safety core
- learning-english-2a6.4: vocabulary MVP
- learning-english-2a6.5: grammar MVP
- learning-english-2a6.6: reading MVP
- learning-english-2a6.7: hardening and release readiness
- learning-english-2a6.8: deferred expansion work

Interpretation guide:
- Parent-child links express phase or scope ownership.
- Task-level blocks edges express actual technical sequencing.
- Top-level phase ordering is documented in comments because br rejected explicit sibling-epic blocks as cycles through the shared parent.
- The graph is intentionally reliability-first: auth and content safety come before breadth.

title: Phase 0: Establish the delivery foundation and implementation scaffold
description:
Convert the repository from a documentation-only plan into an executable workspace. This phase is intentionally about chassis work: repo layout, runtime setup, migrations, app shells, CI, and developer ergonomics.

If this phase is skipped or done loosely, every later module will invent its own structure and conventions and the project will accumulate preventable rework.
design:
- Materialize the target repo shape from PLAN.md instead of improvising a different structure.
- Establish config, local runtime, migrations, and baseline app shells before feature implementation starts.
- Make onboarding predictable enough that a future developer can clone, run, and inspect the system quickly.
acceptance_criteria:
- Frontend/backend/root delivery structure exists.
- Backend and frontend shells can start without feature completeness.
- Migrations and CI have a baseline workflow.
- README/runbooks describe reality rather than aspiration.
notes:
- Keep the scaffold lean but correctly shaped.
- Use this phase to encode the architecture's intended seams early.
- Small upfront rigor here buys down a lot of later churn.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Execution note: get the repo shape, runtime, and workflow right before adding feature code. This is the cheapest point to eliminate structural ambiguity.

Planned child issues in intended execution order:
- learning-english-2a6.1.1
- learning-english-2a6.1.2
- learning-english-2a6.1.3
- learning-english-2a6.1.4
- learning-english-2a6.1.5
- learning-english-2a6.1.6
- learning-english-2a6.1.7
- learning-english-2a6.1.8
- learning-english-2a6.1.9
- learning-english-2a6.1.10

title: Codify architecture guardrails before implementation starts
description:
Translate the most important architectural invariants from PLAN.md and AGENTS.md into implementation-facing guidance before code starts to sprawl.

The repo currently has detailed planning docs but no code, which makes this the ideal moment to turn non-negotiable rules into enforceable conventions.
design:
- Capture hard boundaries: learner/admin API split, revision/publication separation, idempotent writes, deterministic vocab sessions, publication-aware reading progress, and hybrid auth.
- Decide where these rules should live in code structure, not just prose docs.
- Make the guardrails visible enough that a new contributor can feel them immediately.
acceptance_criteria:
- A short implementation-facing architecture guide exists and matches PLAN.md.
- Core package and route boundaries reflect the guardrails.
- The team has explicit guidance on what must not be weakened for convenience.
notes:
- This is the anti-regression issue for the architecture itself.
- The point is to reduce hidden design drift during implementation.
- Prefer a few strong constraints over a large volume of soft advice.

title: Update README and runbooks so the scaffolded project is self-bootstrapping
description:
Rewrite the human-facing setup and workflow docs so they describe the real scaffold, local runtime, commands, and quality checks instead of the current docs-only state.

Good onboarding docs are part of delivery quality because they reduce hidden tribal knowledge and keep the repo honest about what actually exists.
design:
- Document local startup, migrations, seed loading, testing, and common commands.
- Keep the README aligned with the actual scaffold and CI workflow.
- Explain where to look first in the codebase as implementation lands.
acceptance_criteria:
- A new contributor can clone the repo and reach a running local setup from docs alone.
- Commands in docs match reality.
- Onboarding guidance reflects the codebase that exists, not the one that used to exist.
notes:
- Treat docs drift as real technical debt.
- This task should be updated whenever the scaffold changes materially.
- Future selves should not have to reverse-engineer workflow from commit history.

title: Create the repo scaffold that matches the planned backend/frontend layout
description:
Create the top-level structure described in PLAN.md: frontend, backend, and the main delivery files that make the repo feel like an application repo instead of only a planning repo.

The filesystem itself should communicate the intended architecture so later work slots into clear locations instead of inventing folders ad hoc.
design:
- Materialize the target top-level layout with minimal but real entrypoints.
- Keep the scaffold close to the planned architecture instead of introducing a competing convention.
- Avoid over-stubbing every future file; only create what unlocks the next steps.
acceptance_criteria:
- Root directories and key entry files exist.
- Later issues can extend the scaffold without reshaping the repo.
- The structure clearly distinguishes frontend, backend, and delivery concerns.
notes:
- A correct skeleton is more valuable than a large but fake skeleton.
- Treat PLAN.md as the layout source of truth unless it is intentionally revised.
- This issue should reduce later tree churn.

title: Define the environment/config contract for local, CI, and future deployment
description:
Establish how configuration is loaded, validated, and documented across backend, frontend, local development, CI, and future staging/production environments.

Config drift is a classic source of greenfield pain, so make environment shape explicit before services start depending on undeclared variables.
design:
- Define .env.example and runtime config loading patterns for backend and frontend.
- Separate true secrets from ordinary environment toggles.
- Decide how local defaults, CI overrides, and deployment overrides fit together.
acceptance_criteria:
- Developers can discover required env vars from versioned artifacts.
- Runtime startup fails clearly on missing critical configuration.
- The config model supports auth, database, storage, and app URLs without ad hoc sprawl.
notes:
- Validate early and loudly; silent fallback config is a footgun.
- This issue should make later deployment work much less ambiguous.
- Keep naming consistent across code, Compose, CI, and docs.

title: Stand up the Docker Compose local stack with PostgreSQL and app services
description:
Create the local runtime path that lets a developer bring up the database and application services predictably.

The plan explicitly calls out Docker Compose as the baseline for reducing machine drift.
design:
- Add a Compose setup for PostgreSQL and application services.
- Wire service configuration to the config contract rather than hardcoded assumptions.
- Make the local stack good enough for migrations, tests, and manual smoke work.
acceptance_criteria:
- docker-compose up brings up the database and app services coherently.
- Services can connect using documented local defaults.
- The stack is suitable for future smoke checks and onboarding.
notes:
- Favor boring, explicit local infrastructure over clever abstractions.
- This issue reduces cross-machine inconsistency, which the plan calls out as a real risk.
- Keep the local stack close to eventual deployment shape where it helps.

title: Install the SQL migration workflow and bootstrap the database lifecycle
description:
Put a real migration workflow in place before schema work begins so every future schema change travels through versioned, repeatable migrations instead of hand-edited database state.

This is one of the highest-leverage foundation issues because almost every later backend task depends on it.
design:
- Select and wire the migration toolchain for local and CI use.
- Define how migrate up/down commands fit into the Makefile and local stack.
- Ensure the workflow supports module-specific schema additions without chaos.
acceptance_criteria:
- Migrations can be created, applied, and rolled back in a documented way.
- The local stack and CI can run the migration flow.
- Later schema issues have a stable path for landing database changes.
notes:
- This issue should remove any excuse for manual DB patching.
- Keep migrations explicit and reviewable.
- Consistency here will pay off once publication/history tables arrive.

title: Create the backend application shell and package boundaries
description:
Build the minimal Go application shell with the planned package boundaries so later backend work lands into the right shape from day one.

The plan's backend architecture is layered and intentionally explicit; this task makes those layers real instead of aspirational.
design:
- Set up cmd/api and internal with the planned package groups.
- Wire router startup, dependency bootstrap, and middleware registration at a skeletal level.
- Favor a shell that can run and grow over a huge set of empty packages.
acceptance_criteria:
- The backend starts and exposes basic runtime hooks.
- Package layout matches the intended handler/service/repository/query/policy/jobs split.
- Later auth and content-ops issues can slot into stable package boundaries.
notes:
- Keep the shell thin but opinionated.
- The package tree should make it hard to blur query code, policy code, and orchestration.
- This issue is about shape and seams, not feature completeness.

title: Create the frontend application shell with routing, providers, and base layout
description:
Build the minimal React/Vite application shell with routing, providers, layout scaffolding, and the planned feature/module structure.

The goal is for later UI work to extend a coherent shell instead of re-litigating app bootstrapping every time a new page appears.
design:
- Set up the app entrypoint, provider composition, route tree, and feature/module folders.
- Keep learner and admin routes distinct from the start.
- Add a base layout that later pages can inhabit without redesigning app chrome each time.
acceptance_criteria:
- The frontend boots through Vite and renders a base shell.
- Provider and routing structure exists in a way that later pages can reuse.
- Learner/admin route separation is visible in the route tree.
notes:
- Avoid overly decorative UI at this stage; structural correctness matters more.
- This shell should make later auth and feature page work straightforward.
- Keep future persistence and query hooks in mind when placing providers.

title: Set up CI and automation for lint, test, build, and migration checks
description:
Establish automated checks early so the repo does not drift into a state where correctness is only inferred from manual local runs.

The plan expects lint/test/build rigor once the scaffold exists; this issue makes that expectation executable.
design:
- Wire baseline CI jobs for frontend, backend, and migration sanity.
- Start simple, but make the automation reflect the real developer workflow.
- Use this as the backbone for later contract and E2E checks.
acceptance_criteria:
- Pushes or PRs can run baseline checks automatically.
- Frontend/backend/build/migration flows have a documented automation path.
- Later hardening tasks can extend the pipeline rather than invent it.
notes:
- Even a minimal CI pipeline is better than relying on habit.
- Keep job names and outputs clear for future debugging.
- This is the quality gate that later issues will lean on.

title: Create reproducible seed data and sample import artifacts for QA and demos
description:
Provide realistic sample data and import artifacts early so the team can exercise content pipelines and learner flows without hand-editing the database.

Seed data is not just convenience; it is the easiest way to keep manual verification grounded in repeatable scenarios.
design:
- Prepare representative seed users and sample vocabulary, grammar, and reading content.
- Make the artifacts suitable for import preview/publish workflows, not just direct DB insertion.
- Keep the data small enough for fast local setup but realistic enough to reveal UI and workflow issues.
acceptance_criteria:
- Developers can load predictable sample data locally.
- Sample content exercises all major modules and the admin content loop.
- QA and demos can start from reproducible inputs instead of improvised setup.
notes:
- Favor a small, high-signal seed corpus over a large noisy one.
- Include at least one document or lesson shape that stresses edge cases.
- This issue will make later smoke testing much more useful.

title: Phase 1: Build secure auth and session foundations
description:
Build the authentication and session model first because every protected learner route and every admin capability depends on trustworthy identity, role, and session state.

The plan's auth model is stronger than a demo login: refresh-token rotation, DB-backed revocation, and a strict split between short-lived access tokens and long-lived refresh state.
design:
- Implement hybrid auth transport: in-memory access token plus HttpOnly refresh cookie.
- Make session revocation, logout-all, and refresh-token reuse detection first-class design elements.
- Wire backend auth, frontend auth shell, and route protection into one stable contract.
acceptance_criteria:
- Login/me/refresh/logout/logout-all work end-to-end.
- Session rotation and revocation are DB-backed and test-covered.
- Learner/admin route gating is enforceable.
- Frontend protected routes and refresh behavior are stable enough for later feature work.
notes:
- Reuse detection is not optional future hardening.
- Keep access tokens out of localStorage/sessionStorage.
- This phase unlocks both learner and admin surfaces safely.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Execution note: auth is intentionally early because both learner and admin work depend on it. The risky part is the session lifecycle, not the login form.

Planned child issues in intended execution order:
- learning-english-2a6.2.1
- learning-english-2a6.2.2
- learning-english-2a6.2.3
- learning-english-2a6.2.4
- learning-english-2a6.2.5
- learning-english-2a6.2.6
- learning-english-2a6.2.7
- learning-english-2a6.2.8

title: Implement auth schema and migrations for users, sessions, and auth events
description:
Implement the persistence layer behind the auth model: users, user_sessions, and auth_events, including the fields needed for rotation, revocation, and auditability.

This issue makes the auth/session design enforceable at the database level instead of leaving it as service-only convention.
design:
- Add migrations for users, session state, and auth event history.
- Encode the fields needed for token-family tracking, rotation, revocation, and recent usage.
- Add indexes and constraints that match the intended access patterns and safety rules.
acceptance_criteria:
- Auth tables exist and are migratable.
- Session records support revoke-current, revoke-all, and reuse-detection flows.
- Username uniqueness and core auth invariants are enforced at the DB layer.
notes:
- The auth schema is a platform dependency for nearly everything else.
- Prefer explicit fields now over retrofitting session state later.
- Keep naming close to PLAN.md unless the plan changes intentionally.

title: Implement auth security utilities and runtime config for passwords, JWT, and cookies
description:
Build the utility layer for password hashing, JWT signing/verification, cookie policy, and auth configuration.

The plan assumes disciplined handling of these details; this issue prevents them from being improvised inline inside handlers.
design:
- Implement bcrypt helpers, JWT helpers, and config validation for token/cookie settings.
- Keep secrets and cookie policy environment-driven.
- Design the utility layer for rotation-friendly signing and explicit refresh-cookie behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Password hashing and verification are encapsulated cleanly.
- JWT creation and validation are testable and configurable.
- Cookie settings and auth secrets are loaded and validated safely.
notes:
- Avoid scattering crypto details throughout handlers.
- This issue should make later auth code boring and predictable.
- Keep future signing-key rotation in mind even if the first iteration is simple.

title: Implement auth repositories and services for login, me, logout, and logout-all
description:
Implement the core backend business logic for authentication and session lifecycle management.

The service layer needs to make the auth model explicit and testable so handlers remain thin.
design:
- Add repositories for users and sessions plus service methods for the core auth flows.
- Keep transaction boundaries and audit recording coherent.
- Return domain-level outcomes that handlers can translate into the standard API envelope.
acceptance_criteria:
- Login/me/logout/logout-all service paths work against the real schema.
- Repositories hide persistence details from handlers.
- Audit-relevant actions can be recorded consistently.
notes:
- This issue should define the mental model for how auth business logic is organized.
- Keep handlers thin and side effects deliberate.
- Future auth features should extend this layer rather than bypass it.

title: Implement refresh rotation, token-family revocation, and reuse detection
description:
Implement the highest-risk part of the auth design: refresh-token rotation, token family tracking, and reuse detection that can revoke compromised session chains.

The plan explicitly calls out refresh-token reuse detection as part of the intended session model, not as future hardening.
design:
- Rotate refresh tokens on successful refresh.
- Track token family relationships and replaced sessions.
- Detect reuse or revoked-token refresh attempts and revoke the relevant family/state cleanly.
acceptance_criteria:
- Refresh behaves correctly on the happy path.
- Reused or revoked refresh tokens are rejected and handled safely.
- Session lineage is visible enough for auditing and testing.
notes:
- This is easy to ship superficially and still get fundamentally wrong.
- Do not shortcut the lineage or reuse logic just to get demo flows sooner.
- Strong tests here are worth the effort.

title: Implement auth middleware, request user context, and role-based route protection
description:
Build the middleware and policy hooks that enforce authentication and role boundaries on the API surface.

The plan requires a strict separation between public auth routes, private learner routes, and private admin routes.
design:
- Add bearer-token auth middleware and request user/session context loading.
- Provide role-aware protection for learner and admin route groups.
- Keep the policy surface explicit enough that later APIs do not hand-roll authorization checks.
acceptance_criteria:
- Protected routes reject missing or invalid auth cleanly.
- Admin-only routes can be enforced consistently.
- Middleware composition supports later logging, request-id, and security concerns.
notes:
- This is where the learner/admin boundary starts to become enforceable.
- Keep policy decisions visible.
- Prefer shared enforcement over scattered handler checks.

title: Implement auth HTTP handlers and document the auth API contract
description:
Expose the auth services through the planned HTTP contract: login, refresh, logout, logout-all, and me.

This issue is where the backend auth model becomes visible to the frontend and to API consumers.
design:
- Implement handlers, request/response DTOs, and route wiring for the auth endpoints.
- Return the standard success/error envelope defined in PLAN.md.
- Document cookie and bearer-token behavior clearly enough that the frontend can integrate confidently.
acceptance_criteria:
- Auth endpoints are reachable and conform to the intended API design.
- Error mapping is coherent for invalid credentials, revoked sessions, and validation failures.
- The auth contract is clear enough to drive frontend integration and later API docs generation.
notes:
- Thin handlers are the goal here.
- Keep transport-specific behavior at the edge and domain behavior in services.
- This issue should leave behind an auth API that later hardening can document, not reinvent.

title: Implement the frontend auth shell: login page, auth store, refresh gate, and protected routes
description:
Build the frontend side of the auth model so the application can log in, preserve short-lived access state correctly, and protect learner/admin routes.

This issue is where the plan's in-memory access-token strategy and refresh gate become a concrete user experience.
design:
- Implement the login screen, auth store/context, protected route handling, and session refresh flow.
- Keep access token state in memory and rely on refresh cookie transport for renewal.
- Make logout and session-expiry behavior explicit in the UI.
acceptance_criteria:
- Users can log in and reach protected routes.
- Expired access tokens can refresh without page-level confusion on the happy path.
- Failed refresh leads to a clear login recovery path.
notes:
- Avoid localStorage/sessionStorage for long-lived token state.
- This shell should be good enough that later module pages can trust auth plumbing.
- Keep learner/admin route boundaries visible in the route tree.

title: Add auth tests for rotation, revocation, middleware, and frontend session behavior
description:
Add focused auth coverage before other modules depend on the auth layer.

Refresh-token flows can appear to work while still being wrong in edge cases, so freeze intended behavior early.
design:
- Add unit tests for auth services and refresh logic.
- Add integration/API coverage for auth endpoints and middleware.
- Add frontend coverage for protected routes and refresh-failure behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Happy-path auth flows are covered.
- Revoked, reused, and expired-session cases are covered.
- Middleware and frontend auth shell behavior are stable under test.
notes:
- Auth bugs are high-blast-radius bugs.
- This issue should lock down the platform layer beneath later module work.
- Favor realism over shallow mock-driven confidence.

title: Phase 2: Build the content operations and publication safety core
description:
Build the reliability spine of the system: imports, revisions, publications, audit logs, jobs, and idempotency.

The plan is explicit that learner reads must never leak draft state and that publish/rollback must be auditable and retry-safe.
design:
- Implement the common schema and runtime services behind preview, commit, publish, unpublish, rollback, and history inspection.
- Make jobs lease-based and recoverable rather than naive background work.
- Centralize idempotency and publication rules so modules reuse one safety model.
- Deliver admin tooling that makes content changes operationally visible.
acceptance_criteria:
- Draft/source history and live publication history are represented separately.
- Admin operations are retry-safe, auditable, and observable.
- Jobs can recover from worker crashes without silent duplication.
- Admins can inspect imports, jobs, diffs, and publication history.
notes:
- This is the highest-leverage backend phase after auth.
- Weak content-ops implementation would make later features look real but remain operationally fragile.
- Keep the learner/admin boundary strict from the start.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Execution note: this is the reliability spine of the product. If these tasks are weak, later features will look real but remain operationally unsafe.

Planned child issues in intended execution order:
- learning-english-2a6.3.1
- learning-english-2a6.3.2
- learning-english-2a6.3.3
- learning-english-2a6.3.4
- learning-english-2a6.3.5
- learning-english-2a6.3.6
- learning-english-2a6.3.7
- learning-english-2a6.3.8
- learning-english-2a6.3.9

title: Implement the common content-ops schema for imports, revisions, publications, jobs, audit logs, and idempotency
description:
Implement the shared schema that underpins content import, draft history, live publication history, background job execution, auditability, and idempotent mutations.

Later Grammar, Reading, and Vocabulary work all rely on these tables existing and meaning what PLAN.md says they mean.
design:
- Add migrations for imports, import_errors, content_revisions, publications, content_audit_logs, jobs, job_attempts, and idempotency_keys.
- Encode key uniqueness and active-publication invariants where feasible.
- Make the schema clearly express the boundary between draft/source history and live history.
acceptance_criteria:
- Shared content-ops tables exist with the expected relations.
- Active publication and idempotency invariants are represented cleanly.
- Later module-specific publish flows can build on this schema without reinterpretation.
notes:
- This issue gives the rest of the content system a stable contract.
- Prefer strong DB-level guarantees over relying purely on service discipline.
- Keep names and semantics aligned with PLAN.md.

title: Implement the shared idempotency subsystem for learner and admin writes
description:
Implement the common mechanism behind Idempotency-Key handling so important writes can survive retries, reconnects, and impatient re-submission without duplicating side effects.

This shows up in multiple places in the plan: vocab answers, reading progress, import commits, and publish actions.
design:
- Define request hashing, key scoping, persistence, and response snapshot behavior.
- Provide a reusable service/helper boundary so modules do not re-implement idempotency ad hoc.
- Make failure and conflict semantics explicit enough for clients to reason about retries.
acceptance_criteria:
- Reusable idempotency support exists for major mutation paths.
- Duplicate request keys do not create duplicate domain effects.
- Conflict behavior is predictable and testable.
notes:
- This is foundational reliability work, not just API decoration.
- Keep scopes explicit so unrelated actions do not collide accidentally.
- Future module issues should consume this service rather than bypass it.

title: Implement the lease-based jobs engine for commit, publish, unpublish, and rollback
description:
Implement the background execution model for content operations with explicit leases, heartbeats, retries, and dead-letter behavior.

A weak job system would undermine publish safety immediately, so this issue deserves first-class treatment.
design:
- Build queue selection, leasing, heartbeat, retry scheduling, attempt tracking, dedupe-key handling, and dead-letter outcomes.
- Make worker crash recovery an expected path, not an afterthought.
- Expose enough state that admin tooling and observability can explain what happened.
acceptance_criteria:
- Jobs can be claimed safely by workers.
- Crashes or timeouts do not silently strand or duplicate work.
- Attempts and failure reasons are visible for debugging and admin inspection.
notes:
- The main trap here is implementing fire-and-forget background work with no lease model.
- Treat durability and explainability as requirements.
- This issue is a reliability multiplier for every publish-related feature.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Risk note:
- Do not satisfy this issue with in-process fire-and-forget goroutines.
- The whole point is explicit lease ownership, heartbeat, retry visibility, and crash recovery.
- If the implementation cannot explain which worker owns a job and why it failed or retried, it has not met the goal.

title: Implement the artifact storage abstraction for import sources, previews, and validation reports
description:
Create the abstraction that allows import source artifacts, preview payloads, and validation reports to live outside the core relational tables when they grow too large.

The plan calls out artifact URLs as a safety valve for large payloads; this issue keeps that path intentional instead of improvised later.
design:
- Support local/dev-friendly storage now with an interface that can grow into object storage later.
- Make source, preview, and validation artifacts addressable from the imports flow.
- Keep the interface narrow and purpose-built for content-ops artifacts.
acceptance_criteria:
- Import flows can persist and retrieve artifacts through a stable abstraction.
- The system can avoid stuffing oversized preview/report payloads into primary tables.
- Local development can exercise the artifact path without cloud-only dependencies.
notes:
- Prepare the right seam early without overbuilding storage infrastructure.
- The first iteration can be simple if the abstraction is solid.
- Keep artifact metadata obvious in admin workflows.

title: Implement the generic preview and validation framework for content imports
description:
Build the shared import framework that module-specific importers will plug into. The plan assumes a consistent pattern: upload/input -> preview -> validation report -> enqueue commit job.

This issue exists to prevent Grammar, Reading, and Vocabulary from each inventing a slightly different content-ops story.
design:
- Define the shared contracts for preview, validation results, import metadata, and commit-job enqueueing.
- Keep synchronous preview fast and explicit; push long-running work to jobs.
- Make validation output detailed enough for content operators to fix real problems.
acceptance_criteria:
- Module importers can plug into one common preview/validation flow.
- Validation failures are preserved and inspectable.
- Commit remains a distinct job-backed step after successful preview.
notes:
- The real value here is consistency across modules.
- Content operators should not have to learn three different admin mental models.
- This issue should reduce copy-pasted import orchestration code later.

title: Implement publish, unpublish, rollback, and audit services around active publications
description:
Implement the service/policy layer that turns validated revisions into live publications and records the audit trail for publish, unpublish, and rollback actions.

This issue is where the core content-lifecycle idea becomes executable: live content is not overwritten; it advances through publication records.
design:
- Resolve revisions into publication actions with explicit status transitions.
- Update active_publication_id safely and record audit entries and change summaries.
- Make rollback a first-class operation rather than an improvised patch path.
acceptance_criteria:
- Publish/unpublish/rollback flows behave predictably.
- Live history remains reconstructible from publication records.
- Audit logs capture actor, action, and relevant entity context.
notes:
- This issue protects the learner reads only published state rule.
- Favor explicit publication state machines over hidden side effects.
- The system should be explainable after the fact, not just functional in the moment.

title: Implement the admin ops API for imports, jobs, publications, and diff/history inspection
description:
Expose the operational backend surface that content admins need: import history, job status, publication history, and diff/inspection endpoints.

The plan explicitly treats admin tooling as part of the product, not just a convenience script.
design:
- Add protected admin endpoints for imports, jobs, publications, and content diff/history.
- Keep these routes distinct from learner APIs.
- Return enough metadata that the admin UI can explain system state instead of guessing.
acceptance_criteria:
- Admin ops endpoints exist behind admin protection.
- Imports, job status, publication history, and diff metadata are queryable.
- The backend supports a real admin workflow instead of hidden operational steps.
notes:
- This issue is the backend half of making content ops self-service.
- Make API responses operationally descriptive, not just minimally correct.
- Later admin screens should be thin clients over this surface.

title: Implement the admin shell UI for import preview, job tracking, and publication control
description:
Build the admin frontend shell that content operators will actually use: source selection, preview, validation reporting, job monitoring, and publication controls.

The goal is to let content teams operate the system without touching the database directly.
design:
- Create the admin route area and reusable UI pieces for preview/report/history/job status workflows.
- Keep admin workflows clearly separate from learner routes and state.
- Design the UI around clarity and operational trust rather than visual flourish.
acceptance_criteria:
- Admin users can reach a dedicated content-ops shell.
- The UI can show previews, validation failures, job state, and publish controls against the backend APIs.
- The shell is reusable by Grammar, Reading, and Vocabulary import flows.
notes:
- Favor explicit state explanations over clever interactions.
- A content operator should be able to answer what happened from this UI.
- This shell is the reusable frontend substrate for all content modules.

title: Add content-ops tests for draft isolation, retries, auditability, and job recovery
description:
Add focused coverage for the safety properties behind the content-ops core: draft isolation, idempotency, audit logging, and job crash recovery.

These are exactly the areas most likely to look fine in happy-path demos while hiding failures that matter in production.
design:
- Add unit and integration coverage for publication logic, idempotency behavior, and job execution semantics.
- Exercise failure and retry paths deliberately.
- Test that learner reads never resolve draft state.
acceptance_criteria:
- Job retry/recovery behavior is covered.
- Publication and rollback flows are covered.
- Audit and draft-isolation guarantees are covered.
notes:
- These tests should protect the most valuable cross-cutting guarantees in the system.
- Favor realistic state transitions over trivial mocks.
- This issue should make module-level content work much safer to build.

title: Phase 3: Ship the Vocabulary MVP as the first full learner loop
description:
Vocabulary is the first full end-to-end learner loop: content import, publish, learner consumption, progress mutation, retry safety, resume, and session completion.

It is also where the deterministic session model becomes real; the plan explicitly rejects regenerating questions from mutable state on resume.
design:
- Implement the data model, import path, publish path, learner APIs, and learner UI for vocabulary books and study sessions.
- Preserve determinism by snapshotting session items when a session starts.
- Make answer submission idempotent so short network faults do not corrupt progress.
acceptance_criteria:
- Published vocabulary books can be imported, reviewed, published, and consumed by learners.
- Learners can start, resume, answer, and complete sessions without question drift.
- Progress transitions follow the intended new/learning/mastered logic.
- Tests prove retry safety and deterministic resume behavior.
notes:
- Vocabulary should become the reference learner module for write safety.
- Do not take shortcuts that regenerate questions per request.
- A good Vocabulary MVP de-risks later learner modules significantly.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Execution note: Vocabulary is the first full proof that the architecture can support import, publish, learner reads, and retry-safe writes as one coherent loop.

Planned child issues in intended execution order:
- learning-english-2a6.4.1
- learning-english-2a6.4.2
- learning-english-2a6.4.3
- learning-english-2a6.4.4
- learning-english-2a6.4.5
- learning-english-2a6.4.6
- learning-english-2a6.4.7
- learning-english-2a6.4.8
- learning-english-2a6.4.9
- learning-english-2a6.4.10

title: Implement the vocabulary schema, constraints, and migrations
description:
Implement the vocabulary persistence model: books, words, examples, media, per-user progress, sessions, session items, and quiz attempts.

Vocabulary is the first module where learner progress, deterministic sessions, and publication-aware content intersect.
design:
- Add migrations for the vocabulary tables and their indexes.
- Encode uniqueness and ordering rules that the session engine and learner queries will rely on.
- Keep the schema compatible with published content, progress tracking, and deterministic session playback.
acceptance_criteria:
- Vocabulary tables exist with the expected relations and constraints.
- Book/word uniqueness and session-item ordering are enforceable.
- The schema can support publish-time materialization and learner-time progress writes.
notes:
- This schema is deliberately richer than a toy flashcard model.
- Do not optimize away fields that protect determinism or future repetition logic.
- Strong constraints here will simplify later service code.

title: Add vocabulary tests for import, publication, session determinism, and learner flows
description:
Add module-level coverage for the full Vocabulary MVP: import and publication, session generation, answer logic, and learner-facing flows.

Because Vocabulary is the first end-to-end module, its tests also function as an early health check on the overall architecture.
design:
- Cover import and publish behavior, deterministic session generation, progress mutation, and key frontend interactions.
- Add integration and E2E slices where they buy real confidence.
- Protect the retry-safe and resume-safe semantics explicitly.
acceptance_criteria:
- The module has meaningful backend, frontend, and end-to-end coverage.
- Resume/retry determinism is locked down under test.
- Published-only learner reads are verified.
notes:
- Vocabulary tests should become a reference pattern for later module testing.
- High-signal coverage matters more than raw test count.
- This issue closes the loop on the first real learner module.

title: Implement vocabulary CSV/JSON import preview and validation
description:
Implement the vocabulary importer on top of the shared content-ops framework, including column validation, duplicate detection, normalization, and preview generation.

Vocabulary import is the fastest path to proving that content ops can handle structured tabular content without collapsing into manual DB edits.
design:
- Parse CSV/JSON source formats and validate required fields.
- Normalize words/slugs and detect duplicates within a file and against existing content where appropriate.
- Produce a useful preview and validation report for content operators.
acceptance_criteria:
- Valid vocabulary sources can be previewed before commit.
- Invalid data produces actionable validation output.
- Import metadata is preserved for later history and publish flows.
notes:
- The importer should help content teams fix problems quickly.
- Keep normalization rules explicit so downstream logic is stable.
- This issue should integrate naturally with the shared import framework.

title: Implement vocabulary commit and publish materialization from draft revision to learner-visible content
description:
Take validated vocabulary imports through the full lifecycle: commit into draft revision state, publish into learner-visible projections, and maintain publication history correctly.

This issue connects the importer to the shared publication model and makes Vocabulary a real participant in the same safety story as Grammar and Reading.
design:
- Commit validated imports into revisioned vocabulary source state.
- Publish from revision state into the learner-facing tables and relations the module will use.
- Keep active publication tracking and audit behavior aligned with the shared content-ops core.
acceptance_criteria:
- Vocabulary imports can advance from preview to committed revision to active publication.
- Publish does not overwrite history silently.
- Rollback and unpublish semantics fit the shared publication model.
notes:
- Vocabulary should not be a special-case escape hatch around the content-lifecycle model.
- Keep the learner read path sourced from published state only.
- This issue is a major proof point for the whole architecture.

title: Implement published vocabulary queries and learner APIs for books, details, progress, and resume
description:
Implement the learner-facing backend surface for vocabulary: published book listing, book detail, progress lookup, session resume, and supporting queries.

The key rule is that learner reads must resolve from published content only, even though admin workflows and draft revisions exist in the same system.
design:
- Build the query layer and handlers behind vocabulary learner routes.
- Expose book list/detail and the progress/session-resume capabilities the frontend will need.
- Return data in the standard API shape with enough metadata for a smooth UI.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learner APIs for vocabulary books, progress, and resume exist.
- Only published books are returned through learner surfaces.
- Query performance and response shape are good enough for the frontend shell to build on.
notes:
- This issue is about the read side of the module, not session mutation logic.
- Keep read models explicit so session code does not overreach into data-fetch concerns.
- Favor clarity over hyper-generic abstractions.

title: Implement the deterministic vocabulary session engine for sequential, random, and repeat modes
description:
Implement the session-generation logic for vocabulary study, including mode/ordering configuration and snapshotting of issued questions into vocab_session_items.

This is one of the core architectural bets in the plan: the system must not regenerate questions differently on resume or retry.
design:
- Support sequential, random, and repeat ordering strategies with clear tests.
- Snapshot issued items and prompts so resume/retry remain deterministic.
- Keep the strategy layer isolated enough that future repetition logic can evolve without breaking the session contract.
acceptance_criteria:
- Sessions preserve the configuration they started with.
- Resume returns the same logical question sequence instead of regenerating a different one.
- Strategy behavior is testable and explainable.
notes:
- This issue is easy to get superficially right and fundamentally wrong.
- Do not compute the next question solely from current mutable progress state.
- Deterministic session items are the backbone of trustworthy resume/retry behavior.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Risk note:
- Do not generate next question purely from mutable learner progress at request time.
- The contract here is deterministic replay: the same session must be resumable and debuggable.
- vocab_session_items is not incidental data; it is the record that makes the learner experience trustworthy.

title: Implement vocabulary answer submission, progress transitions, and retry-safe attempt recording
description:
Implement the write side of the vocabulary learner loop: answer submission, correctness evaluation, progress updates, and attempt recording with idempotency protection.

This is where learner-visible feedback, stored progress, and retry safety all converge.
design:
- Evaluate answers for MCQ, write, and flip contexts as needed by the module.
- Update user_vocab_progress according to the intended transition rules.
- Record attempts and make answer submission safe under retries and brief network failures.
acceptance_criteria:
- Answer submission updates progress and attempts correctly.
- Duplicate submissions do not create duplicate domain effects.
- The system can power clear learner feedback and reliable resume behavior.
notes:
- Keep the correctness and progress logic explicit and testable.
- Consume the shared idempotency subsystem rather than inventing a vocabulary-only version.
- Small mistakes here will show up directly as learner trust problems.

title: Implement the vocabulary frontend shell: books, detail page, settings, and resume entry points
description:
Build the frontend surfaces around vocabulary discovery and session setup: book list, book detail, study settings, and resume affordances.

This task gives the module its structural UI frame so the actual study interactions have a coherent place to live.
design:
- Implement the vocabulary pages, query wiring, settings UI, and resume entry points.
- Keep the UI aligned with the backend's published-read and session-start contracts.
- Make room for both guess-meaning and learn-word flows without entangling their local state.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learners can browse published books and open a book detail page.
- Study settings and resume affordances are wired to real API contracts.
- The module shell is ready for the detailed study interactions.
notes:
- This issue is about structure and integration, not full interaction polish.
- Keep server state and UI state separated as planned.
- A strong shell will make the session UIs simpler to build and reason about.

title: Implement the guess-meaning MCQ study flow with feedback and progression
description:
Implement the guess-meaning experience: present the prompt, render answer options, submit the choice, show correctness feedback, and advance through the session.

This is the most immediately visible learner interaction in the Vocabulary MVP.
design:
- Render options clearly for mobile and desktop.
- Tie progression and feedback to the real session and answer APIs.
- Handle transient submission failures without corrupting learner state.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learners can complete a functional MCQ session flow.
- Correct and incorrect feedback is clear and aligned with backend results.
- The UI behaves predictably under retries and in-progress state changes.
notes:
- Keep interaction fast and low-friction.
- Do not reimplement correctness logic in the client.
- Favor clarity over flashy transitions.

title: Implement the learn-word flow with flip/write modes, auto-next behavior, and session summary
description:
Implement the second half of the vocabulary learner experience: learn-word mode with flip/write behavior, navigation controls, auto-next, resume friendliness, and end-of-session summary.

This task is where the module becomes a usable study tool rather than only a quiz demo.
design:
- Support flip and write interactions within the session shell.
- Preserve pending input and handle retry-safe submissions where relevant.
- Surface summary information that helps learners understand progress at the end of a session.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learners can study through flip and write flows without losing context.
- Auto-next and summary behavior work coherently with backend state.
- Resume and completion feel stable rather than improvised.
notes:
- Write mode is where transient-network resilience starts to matter a lot.
- This issue should prepare the module for the later persisted-mutation-queue hardening work.
- Keep the UI grounded in the deterministic session model.

title: Phase 4: Ship the Grammar MVP on top of the revision/publication model
description:
Grammar is the first Markdown-heavy module and the cleanest proof that the revision/publication model can support structured instructional content and attached exercises.

This phase operationalizes content import and publishing for human-authored educational material, not just tabular vocabulary data.
design:
- Implement grammar schema, Markdown parsing, revision commit, publication flow, learner APIs, and learner/admin UI.
- Keep exercises and lesson content related but not tightly fused.
- Preserve the rule that learner APIs only read active publications.
acceptance_criteria:
- Admins can preview, validate, commit, publish, and roll back grammar content.
- Learners can browse topics, open lessons, view highlighted keywords, and answer exercises.
- Draft content never leaks into learner routes.
- Tests cover parser correctness, publication flow, and exercise integrity.
notes:
- Grammar is where content authoring ergonomics start to matter.
- Keep the parser strict enough to catch bad content early.
- Publication metadata in learner responses should support freshness and debugging.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Execution note: Grammar validates the Markdown-oriented content path and proves the publication model works for authored instructional content.

Planned child issues in intended execution order:
- learning-english-2a6.5.1
- learning-english-2a6.5.2
- learning-english-2a6.5.3
- learning-english-2a6.5.4
- learning-english-2a6.5.5
- learning-english-2a6.5.6
- learning-english-2a6.5.7

title: Implement the grammar schema, constraints, and migrations
description:
Implement the grammar persistence model for topics, lessons, exercises, options, and learner attempts, with the invariants the plan calls out around topic/lesson relationships and option correctness.

This schema is the structured backbone for Markdown-authored instructional content and attached exercises.
design:
- Add migrations for grammar topics, lessons, exercises, options, and attempts.
- Encode the important invariants around topic alignment and single-correct-option MCQs.
- Keep the schema compatible with the shared revision/publication model.
acceptance_criteria:
- Grammar tables exist with the expected constraints and relations.
- Lesson/topic/exercise integrity is enforceable.
- The module is ready for parser, publish, and learner API work.
notes:
- Use DB constraints where the plan recommends them instead of trusting service logic alone.
- The schema should support both lesson-bound and topic-level exercise use cases.
- Keep names and boundaries aligned with the plan.

title: Implement the grammar Markdown parser, frontmatter validation, and keyword extraction
description:
Build the grammar importer/parser so Markdown lessons can be previewed, validated, and normalized into the canonical structure described in PLAN.md.

This is where the admin content path becomes real for narrative instructional material instead of structured tabular data.
design:
- Parse frontmatter, sections, blocks, and keyword metadata.
- Validate required metadata and lesson/topic linkage assumptions.
- Produce canonical parsed output suitable for preview and revision storage.
acceptance_criteria:
- Valid grammar Markdown can be parsed and previewed.
- Invalid content produces precise validation errors.
- Parsed output carries the data needed for rendering and highlighting.
notes:
- Parser strictness is a product quality feature here.
- Avoid silent coercions that make content problems harder to diagnose later.
- This issue should leave behind a trustworthy import boundary for grammar authors.

title: Implement grammar commit/publish/rollback flow on top of revisions and publications
description:
Connect the grammar parser to the shared content-lifecycle model: commit validated lessons into draft revisions, then publish or roll them back safely for learner use.

This issue validates that Markdown-based authored content can live inside the same publication discipline as the rest of the system.
design:
- Store grammar revisions in the shared revision model.
- Publish into learner-visible lesson state through the shared publication service.
- Preserve history, diffability, and rollback semantics.
acceptance_criteria:
- Grammar content can move through preview, commit, publish, and rollback.
- Learner-visible state resolves through active publications only.
- Publication history remains inspectable and auditable.
notes:
- Do not bypass the shared publication model just because grammar content feels simpler.
- Keep exercise linkage stable across revision and publish steps.
- The main goal is operational trust.

title: Implement grammar learner APIs for topics, lessons, highlights, and exercise submission
description:
Implement the learner-facing grammar backend surface: topic listing, lesson detail, highlight metadata, and exercise-answer submission.

The API should make published lesson rendering and exercise interaction straightforward while preserving the learner/admin split.
design:
- Add learner routes and queries for topics, lessons, and related exercises.
- Include publication metadata where useful for freshness and debugging.
- Implement exercise-answer submission against the grammar attempt model.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learner grammar endpoints exist and return published data only.
- Exercise answers can be submitted and stored.
- The API contract is stable enough for the frontend grammar pages.
notes:
- Keep the API focused on learner needs, not admin internals.
- Publication metadata should aid correctness without overcomplicating clients.
- Thin handlers and explicit query boundaries still matter here.

title: Implement the grammar admin workflow on top of the shared content-ops shell
description:
Build the grammar-specific admin flow on top of the shared admin shell: upload Markdown, preview parse results, inspect validation errors, commit, review diff, and publish or roll back.

This task is how grammar becomes operable by a content team instead of only technically possible through backend endpoints.
design:
- Reuse the common admin shell components where possible.
- Add grammar-specific preview, validation, and publish affordances.
- Make failure states and content errors easy to understand and fix.
acceptance_criteria:
- Admins can run the full grammar content workflow from the UI.
- Validation and job status are visible.
- Publish and rollback controls are integrated with grammar content state.
notes:
- This issue should prove the value of the shared admin shell abstraction.
- Do not hide content problems behind vague UI messaging.
- Operator trust matters more than fancy interaction patterns.

title: Implement the grammar learner UI for topics, lesson rendering, keyword highlights, and exercises
description:
Build the learner-facing grammar pages: topic browsing, lesson rendering via Markdown blocks, highlighted keywords, and exercise interactions.

This is where the imported instructional content becomes a practical learner experience.
design:
- Build topic and lesson pages on top of the published learner APIs.
- Render lesson content and highlight metadata safely.
- Support exercise interaction and feedback in a coherent reading flow.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learners can browse grammar topics and open lessons.
- Lesson content renders correctly with highlights.
- Exercises are integrated into the lesson experience and persist attempts.
notes:
- Preserve readability first; grammar content is instructional text before it is UI chrome.
- Keep the rendering path safe with respect to Markdown/XSS concerns.
- This task should feel clean and dependable on mobile and desktop.

title: Add grammar tests for parser correctness, publication safety, and learner exercise flows
description:
Add module-level coverage for grammar parsing, publishing, learner reads, and exercise flows so Markdown-based content operations remain reliable as the system grows.

Grammar is one of the easiest places for subtle parser drift to leak into user-visible behavior.
design:
- Test frontmatter parsing, block normalization, keyword extraction, and validation failures.
- Test publication safety and draft isolation.
- Test exercise submission and learner rendering paths.
acceptance_criteria:
- Core grammar parser and publish behaviors are under test.
- Learner and admin flows have meaningful coverage.
- The module is protected against content-format regressions.
notes:
- Favor fixture-driven realism over brittle shallow mocks.
- Grammar tests should catch content-authoring mistakes early.
- This issue closes the loop on the grammar MVP.

title: Phase 5: Ship the Reading MVP with publication-aware long-form progress
description:
Reading stress-tests long-form content, bilingual section pairing, section-windowed APIs, and progress updates that must remain correct across publish and rollback changes.

This phase is where publication-aware progress stops being an idea in PLAN.md and becomes an enforced contract.
design:
- Implement schema, importers, publication materialization, learner APIs, learner UI, and progress safety rules for reading documents.
- Keep the content model section-based from the start so large documents remain renderable and resumable.
- Preserve publication context on progress writes to avoid stale overwrite after content changes.
acceptance_criteria:
- Admins can preview, validate, commit, publish, and roll back reading documents.
- Learners can browse documents, read section-based bilingual content, and resume accurately.
- Progress updates are version-aware and publication-aware.
- Tests prove section pairing, publish safety, and stale-write prevention.
notes:
- Reading is the module most likely to surface performance and stale-data problems early.
- Resist flattening long documents into whole-document blob responses.
- The section model is part of the product contract, not just an implementation detail.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Execution note: Reading is where long-form content, section-windowed APIs, and publication-aware progress get their real stress test.

Planned child issues in intended execution order:
- learning-english-2a6.6.1
- learning-english-2a6.6.2
- learning-english-2a6.6.3
- learning-english-2a6.6.4
- learning-english-2a6.6.5
- learning-english-2a6.6.6
- learning-english-2a6.6.7
- learning-english-2a6.6.8

title: Implement the reading schema, constraints, and migrations
description:
Implement the reading persistence model for documents, published sections, highlights, and user progress, including the fields needed for publication-aware resume behavior.

The reading schema is where the plan's long-form, section-based content model becomes concrete.
design:
- Add migrations for documents, sections, highlights, and progress.
- Encode the section-order and progress/version relations the module relies on.
- Keep the schema aligned with publication-scoped learner reads.
acceptance_criteria:
- Reading tables exist and support section-based rendering and progress.
- Unique ordering and progress-tracking constraints are in place.
- The schema is ready for import, publish, and learner API work.
notes:
- The section model is a product requirement, not a storage accident.
- Preserve publication context in the schema so stale-write protection is possible later.
- Strong schema choices here will simplify API and UI design.

title: Implement reading Markdown/plain-text parsers and bilingual section pairing validation
description:
Build the reading importers for Markdown and plain text, including metadata parsing, section splitting, EN/VI pairing, and highlight extraction.

The plan explicitly says pairing errors must block publish; this issue establishes that validation boundary.
design:
- Parse both supported source formats into a canonical section-based structure.
- Validate bilingual pairing and document structure.
- Produce preview-friendly output and actionable validation errors.
acceptance_criteria:
- Valid reading content can be parsed and previewed.
- Broken section pairing is caught before commit or publish.
- Parsed output contains the data needed for publish-time materialization.
notes:
- Section integrity is the most important correctness property in this importer.
- Keep parsing rules explainable to content operators.
- This issue should make bad reading content fail fast and transparently.

title: Implement reading commit/publish/rollback flow with section and highlight materialization per publication
description:
Connect parsed reading content to the shared content-lifecycle model, including publish-time materialization of sections and highlights by publication.

This is how the reading module turns imported source text into stable learner-visible projections.
design:
- Store reading revisions in the shared draft/source history model.
- Materialize sections and highlights against a publication boundary during publish.
- Preserve rollback and history semantics without mutating learner-facing state in place.
acceptance_criteria:
- Reading documents can move from preview to committed revision to active publication.
- Sections and highlights are publication-scoped and queryable.
- Rollback does not destroy or confuse publication history.
notes:
- This issue is central to the module's correctness story.
- Keep the publication boundary explicit so learner progress can reference it safely later.
- Avoid hidden side effects that make section identity ambiguous.

title: Implement reading learner APIs for documents, section windowing, and content retrieval
description:
Implement the learner backend surface for reading document discovery and section-based content retrieval, with APIs designed for long-form rendering rather than whole-document blobs.

The section-windowed API is a core performance and UX assumption in the plan.
design:
- Add document list/detail endpoints and a section-window retrieval endpoint.
- Resolve learner reads through active publications only.
- Return enough metadata for desktop/mobile reader modes and resume behavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learners can discover documents and fetch published sections incrementally.
- APIs avoid whole-document overfetch for long content.
- Response shapes support the planned reading UI modes.
notes:
- Keep the reading module honest about long-content performance.
- Favor explicit pagination/windowing semantics over implicit giant payloads.
- Published-only reads remain a hard rule here.

title: Implement publication-aware reading progress updates with optimistic concurrency and replay safety
description:
Implement reading progress mutation with base_version, publication_id, and section context so stale replays cannot overwrite newer progress or progress tied to a different publication.

This is one of the key reliability-specific ideas in the plan and one of the easiest to get wrong if implemented casually.
design:
- Accept and validate progress updates against version and publication context.
- Reject stale or invalid updates predictably.
- Support a future frontend replay queue without corrupting newer progress.
acceptance_criteria:
- Progress writes are protected against stale overwrite.
- Publication changes do not silently corrupt resume state.
- The API contract is clear enough for offline or pending replay logic on the client.
notes:
- This issue exists specifically to protect against publish/rollback plus replay races.
- Do not reduce it to a simple last-section update endpoint.
- The backend should make stale-write rejection explicit rather than ambiguous.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Risk note:
- This issue exists specifically to prevent stale overwrite after publish/rollback or delayed client replay.
- Treat publication_id and base_version as part of the correctness model, not optional metadata.
- A simple last-write-wins endpoint would violate the architecture even if it appears to work in the happy path.

title: Implement the reading admin workflow on top of the shared content-ops shell
description:
Build the reading-specific admin experience: upload Markdown/plain text, preview section pairing, inspect validation output, and publish or roll back documents safely.

This task makes the reading module operable by content admins instead of only technically supported in the backend.
design:
- Reuse the shared admin shell where possible.
- Surface section pairing and document-structure validation clearly.
- Integrate publish and rollback controls with reading history and job state.
acceptance_criteria:
- Admins can run the full reading content workflow from the UI.
- Pairing problems and job state are visible and understandable.
- Publish and rollback controls are integrated with reading state and history.
notes:
- Make the validation output legible to non-engineer operators when possible.
- This issue should prove that the shared admin shell works for long-form content too.
- Clarity beats UI novelty here.

title: Implement the reading learner UI with section-based rendering, dual-language modes, and resume affordances
description:
Build the learner reading experience for desktop and mobile: document discovery, section-based viewer, language mode switching, toolbar controls, and resume affordances.

This is where the section-windowed API and publication-aware progress model become visible as user experience.
design:
- Implement document list/detail surfaces and the main reading viewer.
- Support desktop dual-language reading and mobile language toggling without requiring pixel-perfect sync.
- Integrate progress save and resume behavior cleanly.
acceptance_criteria:
- Learners can open and read section-based published documents.
- Desktop and mobile reading modes both function coherently.
- Resume behavior uses the real progress API and publication context.
notes:
- The goal is robust readability, not fancy interaction experiments.
- Keep the section abstraction visible in the UI model because it underpins performance and correctness.
- This task should feel stable on long content, not just short examples.

title: Add reading tests for parser correctness, publish safety, progress concurrency, and learner flows
description:
Add module-level coverage for reading import, publication, progress mutation, and section-based learner behavior.

Reading has a higher-than-average risk of subtle stale-data, pairing, and long-content regressions.
design:
- Test format parsing, section pairing, publication materialization, and progress mutation logic.
- Test learner API and UI behavior on long-form content paths.
- Protect rollback and replay-safety semantics specifically.
acceptance_criteria:
- The module has meaningful backend, frontend, and end-to-end coverage.
- Pairing and progress safety guarantees are under test.
- Learner reading flows are protected against the regressions most likely to hurt real users.
notes:
- This issue closes the loop on one of the most operationally subtle modules.
- Favor realistic fixtures and publication transitions.
- The goal is confidence in correctness, not just high coverage numbers.

title: Phase 6: Harden quality, operations, and release readiness
description:
This phase turns the system from a feature-complete MVP into something a team can run with confidence.

Logging, health checks, OpenAPI/client sync, observability, E2E smoke coverage, deployment flows, and release acceptance all live here.
design:
- Finish the cross-cutting operational work that makes the system observable, secured, tested, and deployable.
- Close the gap between module-level success and whole-system confidence.
- End with an explicit MVP acceptance pass instead of assuming completeness.
acceptance_criteria:
- Shared runtime concerns are implemented and testable.
- API contracts, client usage, and deployment workflows are aligned.
- Core learner/admin loops have E2E smoke coverage.
- MVP scope is checked explicitly against PLAN.md before declaring success.
notes:
- This is not optional polish; it is the difference between prototype and operational system.
- Favor measurable readiness signals over subjective confidence.
- Leave behind a project that future contributors can run, verify, and extend safely.

comments.author: Codex
comments.text:
Execution note: these tasks are not ornamental polish. They are the work that turns a feature-complete system into something a team can operate and trust.

Planned child issues in intended execution order:
- learning-english-2a6.7.1
- learning-english-2a6.7.2
- learning-english-2a6.7.3
- learning-english-2a6.7.4
- learning-english-2a6.7.5
- learning-english-2a6.7.6
- learning-english-2a6.7.7
- learning-english-2a6.7.8
- learning-english-2a6.7.9

title: Implement shared runtime concerns: request IDs, logging, recovery, CORS, healthz, and readyz
description:
Implement the shared runtime layer the plan expects on the backend: request IDs, structured logging, panic recovery, CORS handling, and health/readiness endpoints.

These features make the system diagnosable and operable, especially once multiple modules and job workers are active.
design:
- Add core middleware and endpoint wiring for runtime diagnostics.
- Make request correlation and error reporting consistent.
- Keep the runtime layer reusable across learner and admin routes.
acceptance_criteria:
- Requests carry stable correlation metadata.
- Panic recovery and readiness/health endpoints exist.
- Logging/CORS/runtime behavior is consistent across the app.
notes:
- This issue provides the observability floor the rest of the system will build on.
- Keep logs structured and purposeful.
- Health and readiness should reflect real system state, not cosmetic success.

title: Implement security hardening for CORS/CSRF, input validation, sanitization, upload checks, and privileged actions
description:
Implement the cross-cutting security protections the plan calls out: careful CORS/CSRF handling, request validation, Markdown sanitization, upload constraints, and stronger handling around privileged admin actions.

The point is to make the platform safe by design, not only by developer caution.
design:
- Tighten request validation and sanitization across import and render paths.
- Apply the right protections for cookie-backed refresh/logout flows.
- Make admin-sensitive actions explicit and defensible.
acceptance_criteria:
- Core security controls are implemented and testable.
- Markdown/content render paths are not casually XSS-prone.
- Admin-sensitive operations have the expected guardrails.
notes:
- This issue should close the obvious gap between a working system and a safe one.
- Favor explicit denial and validation over permissive defaults.
- Security concerns should be surfaced in code structure, not buried in ad hoc conditionals.

title: Implement persisted frontend mutation queues and draft recovery for retry-sensitive workflows
description:
Implement the client-side persistence and replay model for the workflows most sensitive to transient network problems: vocabulary write-mode answers, reading progress, and admin import drafts.

The plan explicitly wants short network issues not to destroy user input or progress.
design:
- Add persisted queues or draft storage for the high-value mutation paths.
- Replay against the existing idempotency and optimistic-concurrency contracts.
- Make pending state visible enough that users are not confused about what has or has not been saved.
acceptance_criteria:
- Pending mutation recovery works for the targeted workflows.
- Replay uses the real backend safety contracts.
- Users do not silently lose important input during short outages or refreshes.
notes:
- Keep the offline/pending model deliberately scoped; not every interaction needs persistence.
- This issue should make reliability tangible to learners and admins.
- Avoid a magic queue that is impossible to inspect or debug.

title: Generate OpenAPI docs, sync the TypeScript client, and add contract drift checks
description:
Make the API contract executable by generating OpenAPI output, using it to drive frontend client sync where appropriate, and adding drift checks in CI.

This is one of the cleanest ways to keep the backend/frontend boundary honest as the project grows.
design:
- Generate and version API docs for the implemented endpoints.
- Use the docs to support or generate a frontend client contract.
- Add CI checks that catch meaningful contract drift early.
acceptance_criteria:
- API documentation reflects the implemented surface.
- Frontend/backend contract drift becomes visible in automation.
- The project has a sustainable path for evolving its API with less guesswork.
notes:
- Keep the docs grounded in the real system, not stale aspirations.
- The point is reduced ambiguity, not bureaucracy.
- This issue should lower integration friction materially.

title: Implement observability signals for APIs, jobs, imports, publish flows, and learner reliability metrics
description:
Add the metrics and operational signals the plan calls for around auth failures, API latency, import/publish behavior, job retries, and learner-facing reliability signals.

Observability is what turns failure from a mystery into something the team can reason about and improve.
design:
- Emit and aggregate the highest-value metrics and structured events.
- Make jobs, imports, publish flows, and high-value learner writes observable.
- Keep dashboards and alerts focused on the failure modes that matter to this product.
acceptance_criteria:
- Operators can observe the system's most important reliability and throughput signals.
- Content-ops and learner-path failures are measurable.
- The metrics support debugging and release-readiness decisions.
notes:
- Start with the signals the plan already names instead of instrumenting everything equally.
- Good observability is selective and actionable.
- This issue should help the team notice regressions before users do.

title: Implement end-to-end smoke coverage for the core learner and admin loops
description:
Add end-to-end smoke coverage for the flows the project cares about most: auth, vocabulary session flow, grammar learning, reading, and admin import/commit/publish.

This validates that the full stack works together under realistic conditions, not just isolated unit assumptions.
design:
- Cover the key learner and admin happy paths and a few high-value failure paths.
- Use the seeded content/data strategy so runs are reproducible.
- Keep the suite focused on confidence, not exhaustive UI microbehavior.
acceptance_criteria:
- Core MVP flows have automated smoke coverage.
- Tests can run in CI or a repeatable local environment.
- The suite catches integration breakage across backend, frontend, and content ops.
notes:
- The aim is fast, high-signal coverage.
- Resist building a brittle giant suite before the core paths are stable.
- This issue is a major input into final MVP acceptance.

title: Tune hot paths for query efficiency, section windowing, lazy loading, and large-content UX
description:
Address the highest-value performance risks called out in PLAN.md: database indexes, N+1 avoidance, reading section windowing, lazy loading, and learner UX on large content.

This issue exists so performance work is deliberate and tied to known hotspots rather than hand-wavy last-minute tweaking.
design:
- Profile and tighten the main query and rendering hotspots.
- Add indexes and query improvements where the plan already anticipates pressure.
- Keep reading and vocabulary experiences responsive on realistic data sizes.
acceptance_criteria:
- Known hot paths have been reviewed and improved where needed.
- Long-form reading avoids obvious overfetch or over-rendering problems.
- The system's p95 behavior is materially better understood and improved.
notes:
- Optimize where the architecture suggests real value, not everywhere equally.
- Prefer clarity-preserving wins over clever complexity.
- Observability should help drive this work.

title: Implement deployment scripts/manifests and environment promotion flow for staging and production
description:
Define how the application moves beyond local development: build artifacts, environment promotion, migrations, rollout steps, and the basic staging/production deployment path.

The plan assumes normalized deployment, not hand-run heroics.
design:
- Define the build and deploy steps plus migration order for non-local environments.
- Keep environment-specific config and secrets handling aligned with the earlier config contract.
- Make staging/prod deployment reproducible enough for the team to trust.
acceptance_criteria:
- The project has a documented and executable non-local deployment path.
- Schema migration and app rollout order are explicit.
- Staging/prod assumptions are no longer implicit tribal knowledge.
notes:
- This issue should eliminate avoidable deployment ambiguity.
- Favor boring, reviewable deployment workflows.
- Keep rollback and operational safety in mind even if the first deployment path is simple.

title: Run the MVP acceptance pass against PLAN.md and close the final gaps intentionally
description:
Perform an explicit acceptance pass against the MVP scope and smoke checklist in PLAN.md instead of assuming that a collection of completed issues automatically adds up to a coherent release.

This issue forces a final, criteria-based review of whether the project has actually delivered what it set out to deliver.
design:
- Walk the implemented system against the MVP scope, roadmap exits, and smoke checklist.
- Identify and close high-value gaps intentionally.
- Produce a clear done/not-done judgment with supporting evidence.
acceptance_criteria:
- The MVP scope has been reviewed explicitly against the implemented system.
- Any remaining gaps are either fixed or intentionally deferred.
- Release readiness is backed by evidence rather than optimism.
notes:
- This issue is where the project proves discipline.
- Avoid redefining success quietly at the end.
- The outcome should be clear enough that future selves know what was truly shipped.

title: Post-MVP: Capture deliberate expansion work without polluting MVP scope
description:
Capture the major post-MVP expansions already named in PLAN.md so they remain visible without pressuring the MVP delivery path.

Explicitly parking these ideas is healthier than pretending they do not exist or allowing them to leak into core delivery prematurely.
design:
